coredata.dc:title: Organic-inorganic face-to-face ZnO NRs-based self-powered UV photodetectors: Heterojunction with poly(3,4-ethylenedioxyselenophene) and enhanced responsivity by carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Serkjan, Nawrzhan
Liu, Xiong
Abdiryim, Tursun
Liu, Fangfei
Zhang, Hujun
Kadir, Aygul
Liu, Yajun
Tang, Xinsheng
Cheng, Qian
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel organic-inorganic self-powered UV photodetector (UV PD) is realized by the face-to-face assembling of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxyselenophene) (PEDOS)/FTO and ZnO nanoarrays (ZnO NRs)/FTO. The ZnO NRs are further modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to achieve high-performance capability of UV PD. As a hole transport material, PEDOS can enhance the charge transfer ability and form p-n heterojunction with ZnO NRs, which can separate the electron-hole pairs under the effect of the built-in electric field, making the UV PD achieve better photoelectric performance without external bias. In addition, a pyroelectric effect is found between ZnO NRs and CQDs and can greatly improve the photoresponsivity and shorten the response time of UV PD. The UV PD from CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS has the excellent performance with photoresponsivity of 22.62 mA W−1 and response speed of 0.09 s/0.12 s (rise time/decay time) under the low ultraviolet light intensity (0.32 mW cm−2, wavelength 365 nm) at 0 V bias. This work provides an effective route to modulate the performance of UV PDs.

head.ce:title: Organic-inorganic face-to-face ZnO NRs-based self-powered UV photodetectors: Heterojunction with poly(3,4-ethylenedioxyselenophene) and enhanced responsivity by carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830017, Xinjiang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830017, Xinjiang, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel organic-inorganic self-powered UV photodetector (UV PD) is realized by the face-to-face assembling of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxyselenophene) (PEDOS)/FTO and ZnO nanoarrays (ZnO NRs)/FTO. The ZnO NRs are further modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to achieve high-performance capability of UV PD. As a hole transport material, PEDOS can enhance the charge transfer ability and form p-n heterojunction with ZnO NRs, which can separate the electron-hole pairs under the effect of the built-in electric field, making the UV PD achieve better photoelectric performance without external bias. In addition, a pyroelectric effect is found between ZnO NRs and CQDs and can greatly improve the photoresponsivity and shorten the response time of UV PD. The UV PD from CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS has the excellent performance with photoresponsivity of 22.62 mA W−1 and response speed of 0.09 s/0.12 s (rise time/decay time) under the low ultraviolet light intensity (0.32 mW cm−2, wavelength 365 nm) at 0 V bias. This work provides an effective route to modulate the performance of UV PDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Self-powered UV PDs with high responsivity and fast sensitivity have attracted extensive attention in various practical applications, for instance, optical communication, flame detection, environmental monitoring, and space communication [1–3]. Conventional UV PDs typically rely on an external DC voltage to achieve photoinduced electron-hole pair separation [4–7], which is not optimal for remote, long-term unmaintained sensing applications. Compared with traditional UV PDs, the advantage of self-powered UV PDs is that it can quickly separate electron-hole pairs through a built-in electric field without relying on an external DC voltage [8,9], which can point the way for future development of UV PDs.

In recent years, organic-inorganic hybrid heterojunctions of high-performance self-powered UV PDs are widely investigated, in which the organic materials with tunable and easy processing features are combined with inorganic materials with broadband absorption and excellent eigenstate carrier mobility [10–12]. Organic-inorganic heterostructures generally generate photovoltaic effect by staggered energy band arrangement between p-type organic semiconductor materials and n-type inorganic semiconductor materials, which significantly promotes the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and contributes to the self-powered performance. In this context, conductive polymers with a high π-conjugation, such as polypyrrole (PPy), poly(3,4-ethylenedioxy thiophene) (PEDOT) and polyaniline (PANI)， are developed for self-powered UV PDs [13–16]. Due to their superior hole transport capabilities, they are utilized as p-type semiconductor materials. ZnO is usually used as one of the preferred n-type inorganic semiconductor materials for UV PDs [17–20]. It has the advantages of high photoelectron transfer efficiency, environmental friendliness, and low manufacturing cost. More importantly, ZnO is a wide direct bandgap (3.37 eV) semiconductor with large exciton binding energy of 60 meV [21,22], which is a promising contender in the field of UV PDs. The p-n heterojunction between organic-inorganic heterojunction semiconductors is helpful to enhance the light response [23–25]. It is reported that the relatively large depletion region of p-n heterostructures can generate more carriers, which are separated by the built-in electric field to form a photocurrent without any external voltage [26,27]. For example, Chen et al. [28] prepared a ZnO/PANI inorganic-organic heterojunction UV photodetector. Under the self powered condition, the responsivity of the device is 0.56 mA W−1, and a rapid response time of 0.11 ms/1.45 ms (rise time/delay time). Boruah et al. [8] prepared a PEDOT:PSS/Cl-doped ZnO NRs self-powered UV PD with a responsivity of 0.80 mA W−1 under 365 nm UV light.

The photoinduced pyroelectric effect is considered as a main factor to improve the performance of self-powered UV PDs [29,30]. Pyroelectric effect is an interface physical effect, and the polarized charges are generated in the material under the self induction of light, which can effectively regulate the transmission and separation of local carriers [31–33]. It is reported that ZnO with wurtzite structure is a good candidate as pyroelectric material, and the pyroelectric potential appears in the ZnO under the temperature change caused by light, which is coupled with the built-in potential to accelerate the separation and transmission of carriers under self-powered mode [34–37]. In addition, further modification of ZnO with carbon-based materials [3] (carbon quantum dots, graphene, carbon nanotubes, etc.) can effectively enhance the pyroelectric effect. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are one of the important carbon-based materials [38], and it has been used as a potential photosensitive material due to its unique electron transfer ability [39–41], photostability, biocompatibility and environmental friendliness. Therefore, the interface interaction between carbon quantum dots and ZnO NRs can further enhance the pyroelectric effect and photoelectric effect, providing a new possibility for the application of ultrafast light detection. For instance, Sinha et al. [42] modified ZnO NRs with carbon dots to fabricate a flexible self-powered UV PD with the responsivity of 9.57 mA W−1 and the rise and fall times of 2 s/3.2 s at a light intensity of 20 mW cm−2. Qiao et al. [43]. have prepared ZnO/PEDOT: PSS heterojunction devices. The responsivity of this device is 0.199 mA/W under zero bias voltage. In addition, under the effect of pyroelectric effect, the optical response is greatly improved, with an increment of 1589%. It should be noted that the high responsivity of these photodtectors are resulting from applying of high light intensity, and the response time still need to be improved.

In this work, we proposed a new solution to construct ZnO-based self-powered UV PDs with high photoresponsivity and short response time. The two pieces of FTO glass (PEDOS/FTO, ZnO NRs/FTO) were assembled by a face-to-face strategy to realize the organic-inorganic heterojunction for UV PDs. In addition, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used for the further modification of ZnO NRs to form a face-to-face construction of FTO/PEDOS with CQDs-ZnO NRs/FTO to investigate the contribution of CQDs on the photodetection ability of UV PD. Befitting from the p-n heterojunction (between PEDOS and ZnO NRs) and the pyroelectric effect (between CQDs and ZnO NRs), the UV PD from CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS had the excellent performance with photoresponsivity of 22.62 mA W−1 and response speed of 0.09 s/0.12 s (rise time/decay time) under the low ultraviolet light intensity (0.32 mW cm−2, wavelength 365 nm) at 0 V bias.

ZnO NRs were prepared on FTO substrate using a hydrothermal method (see Supporting Information for detailed preparation procedures).The CQDs were spin-coated five times on the surface of ZnO NRs/FTO with the spin-coater at 3000 r min−1 for 30 s. After then, the ZnO NRs/FTO substrate coated with CQDs was placed at 110 °C for 30 min in a vacuum oven to obtain FTO/CQDs-ZnO NRs.

PEDOS/FTO was prepared by the electropolymerition of EDOS monomer on the FTO substrate using CV mode. A solution of 0.01 м EDOS and 0.05 м tetra-n-butylammonium hexafluorophosphate(TBAPF6) in dichloromethane was prepared as electrolyte. Electrochemical polymerization was performed using an electrochemical instrument (CHI760E) and a three-electrode electrochemical cell, where the working, counter, and reference electrodes were FTO, platinum, and Ag/AgCl, respectively. In CV mode, the voltage range is 0–1.6 V, and the scan rate is 50 mV s−1 for four cycles. Finally, FTO substrate with PEDOS was washed with dichloromethane and dried under the air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV PD device fabrication and optoelectronic measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared FTO/CQDs-ZnO NRs and PEDOS/FTO were attached face to face, then fixed with high temperature tape, and then evenly coated with PVA on the device as a protective layer. The effective illumination area of all the fabricated devices is 0.24 cm2. The electrochemical performance of the device was tested under the light illumination using electrochemical workstation(CHI760E). The light source for the test was a 500 W mercury light source(Perfectlight, CHF-XM500 M) with a 365 nm filter. Light intensity was measured with a Lutron UV-340A power meter. All measurements were performed at room temperature.

The morphology and structure of the as-prepared materials were characterized by SEM (S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). The optical masers of the laser at 532 nm were characterized by Raman spectroscopy (HR Evolution, Bruker, Germany). Further XRD (AXS-D8, Bruker, Germany) was used to analyze the crystal structure of the material. The materials were analyzed for light absorption using ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV-2550, Shimadzu Corporation).

The face-to-face assembly process of the organic-inorganic heterojunction device is depicted in Fig. 1 . ZnO NRs grown by hydrothermal method are highly ordered perpendicular to the FTO conductive glass substrate. The ZnO NRs have an average length of about 1.2 μm and a diameter of about 100 nm, and this high-density nanoarray helps to amplify the photogenerated current (Fig. 2 a). Electrochemical polymerization was used to polymerize EDOS monomer on FTO substrate. It is observed in Fig. 2b that the PEDOS film has a dense surface composed of particles. The inset is a cross-sectional view of the polymer on FTO substrate. The thickness of the polymer film is about 300 nm, indicating that the electropolymerization of EDOS monomer tends to form dense polymer film, which are favorable for wonderful contact with ZnO NRs. As shown in FT-IR spectra of EDOS and PEDOS (Fig. S1, Supporting Information), the peaks of PEDOS broadens apparently than that of EDOS, and the disappearance of C–H vibration at 3115 cm−1 from the 2,5-position of the selenophene ring in EDOS, suggesting the formation of PEDOS film [68].

Fig. 2c shows the EDS and Mapping diagram of CQDs-ZnO NRs composite. The content of O element (44.68%) is greater than that of Zn element (38.76%), because the O element not only comes from ZnO, but also includes the carboxyl O on the surface of CQDs. The Zn element only comes from ZnO. From the uniform distribution of element C in the Mapping diagram, it can be judged that the CQDs are evenly distributed on ZnO.

In order to further understand the interaction between ZnO NRs and CQDs, the ZnO NRs and CQDs-ZnO NRs complexes were tested by XPS. The total spectrum in Fig. 2d shows that there are zinc (Zn 2p), oxygen (O 1s) and carbon (C 1s) in the CQDs-ZnO NRs composite. In Fig. 2e, there are two symmetrical peaks of Zn 2p spectrum at 1021.18 eV and 1044.30 eV corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of ZnO, respectively. Compared with the Zn2p spectrum of pure ZnO, the Zn2p spectrum of the composite shifted 0.30 eV towards the direction of high binding energy, which is attributed to the force between ZnO and CQDs. The main peak of C 1s spectrum in Fig. 2g at 284.86 eV corresponds to the C–C bond of sp2 orbital. The peaks at 286.51 eV and 288.41 eV correspond to C–O bond and CO bond respectively [44,45], indicating that carbon oxygen bond exists in the composite. In Fig. 2f, the peaks at 530.06 eV and 531.58 eV of the pure ZnO O1s spectrum correspond to the O2− in the ZnO lattice and the defect O2− in the ZnO [46], respectively. From the O 1s spectrum of CQDs-ZnO NRs, the original O2− peak of ZnO defect shifted from 531.58 eV to 532.06 eV, and the peak became significantly stronger, which indicated that the oxygen vacancy in the ZnO lattice was occupied by the carboxyl group of CQDs to form a Zn–COO bond [47,48], further proving the role between ZnO NRs and CQDs.

Fig. 3 a is the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CQDs-ZnO NRs and pure ZnO NRs samples, and the diffraction peaks at 31.90°, 34.60°, 36.43°, 47.67°, 63.09° and 72.60° correspond to ZnO (100), (002), (101), (102), (103) and (004) planes, respectively, indicating that the obtained ZnO has a pure hexagonal wurtzite structure, which is consistent with the standard spectrum of hexagonal wurtzite ZnO (JCPDS no. 36–1451) [49,50]. Except for these characteristic peaks, some additional peaks in the XRD spectra are attributed to the diffraction peaks of the FTO substrate, and the strong diffraction peak for (002) suggests that the ZnO grow along the direction perpendicular to the C-axis of the FTO substrate. This is consistent with the results of SEM analysis. In the XRD patterns of CQDs-ZnO NRs, no obvious signals of CQDs are detected. Therefore, in order to verify the presence of CQDs in the composites, the materials are subjected to Raman testing at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. Fig. 3b demonstrates the Raman spectra of pure ZnO NRs and CQDs-ZnO NRs. From the Raman spectrum of pure ZnO NRs, a extremely strong peak at 436 cm−1 can be observed, which corresponds to the E2 high-frequency vibration mode. The width of this peak is very narrow, indicating that the crystallization of the prepared ZnO NRs is excellent. The weak peak at 380 cm−1 comes from the A1(TO) vibrational mode [21], and the broad peak around 1125 cm−1 is derived from the secondary peak of E1L (LO) [51,52]. From the Raman spectra of CQDs-ZnO NRs, it is evident that except the characteristic peaks of ZnO NRs, there are peaks at 1330 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 corresponding to the D and G spectra of carbon in CQDs [53], respectively. Fig. 3c shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of pure ZnO NRs and CQDs-ZnO NRs. Among them, pure ZnO NRs have strong absorption peaks between 300 nm and 400 nm due to their own band edge absorption. After introducing CQDs, the band edge absorption of CQDs-ZnO NRs shows a red shift and its intensity increases, which is attributed to the conjugation between ZnO NRs and CQDs. The result indicates that CQDs-ZnO NRs are more sensitive to light and have better optical properties.

For better explanation and comparison, we fabricate four devices of UV PDs: FTO/ZnO NRs/FTO, FTO/CQDs-ZnO NRs/FTO, FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO and FTO/CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO. As presented in Fig. 4 , the self-powered photoresponse behaviors of four devices under 365 nm UV light irradiation with a power density of 0.32 mW cm−2 are investigated. Fig. 4a–d are the I–V plots of the four devices, showing the change of light-dark current under 0 bias, demonstrating that all four devices prepared have self-powered characteristics. However, the dark current of FTO/ZnO NRs/FTO and FTO/CQDS ZnO NRs/FTO devices is higher than that of FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO and FTO/CQDS ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO devices. The existence of dark current is attributed to the leakage current formed by the surface defects of ZnO crystal. After the addition of PEDOS, the carrier concentration in the depletion region and the dark current caused by carrier thermal diffusion will be increased [54,55].

Fig. 4e is a multi-cycle I-t diagram of pure ZnO NRs. It is observed in Fig. 4i that pure ZnO NRs display a four-stage photoresponse behavior denoted by Roman numerals I, II, III, and IV, respectively. The sharp output current is clearly seen in the stage I, which is due to the thermal photoelectrons generated by transient pyroelectricity induced by the photoradiation energy and the photogenerated carriers excited by the UV light to the wide-bandgap ZnO NRs [35,36], resulting the current of the device reaches the maximum value instantaneously, and the maximum output current is called I pyro + photo . In stage II, since there is no continuous temperature change, the change of the instantaneous temperature during illumination gradually tends to be stable and becomes the thermal effect. Therefore, the total output current in the stage II is the I photo + thermo dominated by photovoltaic effect. In the stage III, because the pyroelectric effect of ZnO NRs at low power density is not too sensitive, and the sharp peak in the stage I is weak, the reverse pyroelectric effect has not yet appeared when the light in the stage III is turned off (Fig. S6b, Supporting Information).The rise time (τrise) and fall time (τfall) of the devices prepared by pure ZnO NRs are calculated by 0.203 s/0.478 s.

The I-t test on the CQDs sensitized ZnO NRs(CQDs-ZnO NRs) device is shown in Fig. 4f, and it is found that the CQDs sensitized device exhibit an obvious four-stage photoresponse behavior(Fig. 4j). Compared with that of pure ZnO NRs device, it is interesting that the I pyro + photo output current induced by the transient pyroelectric and photoelectric effects in the stage I is sharper in CQDs-ZnO NRs based device under light irradiation. This can be attributed to the role of CQDs, which act as excellent electron donors and acceptors [69], and the coupling with ZnO NRs can achieve better efficient separation of electrons and holes [47,70]. The appearance of sharp output current is more dependent on the thermionic effect of CQDs, because of CQDs can easily absorb photon energy under light irradiation to cause the pyroelectric effect, which is thermal polarization going the same way as the photocurrent produced by the photovoltaic effect, resulting in the appearance of sharp output current(Fig. S6e, Supporting Information). The stage II is similar to that of pure ZnO NRs, along with the gradual decrease of the pyroelectric current, the output current is also greatly reduced, and then returns to the stable value I photo + thermo. In the stage III, the photocurrent disappears when the UV irradiation is turned off. Since CQDs have a certain electron storage capacity, the instantaneous temperature drop leads to reverse thermal polarization, and the device displays a sharp reverse output current I pyro'+thermo’. In addition, the rise time (τrise)/fall time (τfall) of CQDs-ZnO NRs is 0.078 s/0.077 s, which is greatly shortened compared with that of pure ZnO NRs. Fig. 4g is the I-t curve of the FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO device. Compared with the CQDs-ZnO NRs device, the output current I pyro + photo in the stage I is obviously weakened, and there is no sharp output current peak in Fig. 4k. On the contrary, the I-t curve characteristics of the FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO device are similar to that of the pure ZnO NRs device. However, compared with FTO/ZnO NRs/FTO device, the pyroelectric effect of FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO in the first stage is significantly weakened (Fig. S6b, Supporting Information), and the device is more insensitive to the change of instantaneous temperature, but the photovoltaic effect of device plays a leading role. This is attributed to PEDOS which is a typical π-conjugated conductive polymer with high hole transport ability [71], and can form a p-n heterojunction with ZnO NRs generating a built-in electric field in the junction area. The Se–Se interaction provides enhanced interchain charge transfer [15], and it promotes the separation of photogenerated carriers by preventing combination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Consequently, this greatly improve the output current, leading the output current of the FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO device increase by 14 times as compared with that of the pure ZnO NRs device (Fig. S2, Supporting Information).

Finally, we perform I-t tests on the FTO/CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO composite device(Fig. 4h), and it indicates that the existence of the pyroelectric effect (between ZnO NRs and CQDs) and the photocurrent generated by the synergistic effect of ZnO NRs and PEDOS (Fig. 4l). Compared with pure ZnO NRs, the device prepared by FTO/CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO exhibits a 33-fold increase in responsivity (Fig. S3, Supporting Information).

In order to discuss the self-powered working mechanism of FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO devices in detail, the photoresponses of different power densities are measured under 365 nm UV light irradiation. Fig. 5 a displays the I-t characteristic curves of the devices at different power densities from 0.32 to 2.40 mW cm−2 at zero bias. With the increase of power density, a sharp output current peak appears in the stage I, which is due to the sharp rise in temperature of ZnO NRs caused by strong optical radiation. When the power density increases to 2.40 mW cm−2, the sharp peak in the stage I is more obvious, but the reverse sharp current peak does not appear in the stage III. This is due to the fact that the shape of PEDOS and ZnO NRs is not sensitive to the reduction of the instantaneous temperature when the light is turned off, and it is not suitable for heat dissipation. Obviously, the reverse pyroelectric effect in the stage III does not exist. This phenomenon can be seen from the multiple cycle I-t curve under the strong power density of 2.40 mW cm−2(Fig. 5a). When the first cycle was conducted, the pyroelectric effect in the stage I is very strong. When the second cycle is conducted, it has no time to dissipate heat [56], so the temperature difference between the two cycles is not obvious, leading to the sharp peak in the stage I of the next cycle being significantly weakened. In Fig. 5b, the relative peak-to-peak current increases with the increase of power density, and the photocurrent also increases greatly. However, with the increase of power density, the change of I photo + thermo current is not obvious. This is due to the fact that in the stage Ⅱ, I photo current is the main current, while the strong power density light will cause the shielding effect of photogenerated carriers in the ZnO NRs layer and the saturation of photovoltaic effect [26,27], so I photo + thermo does not show too much change with the increase of power density(Fig. 5b). The photoresponsivity (R) and specific detection rate (D*) of the devices(Fig. 5c and d) under different power densities are calculated. The R value is calculated as follows:

Among them, e is the primary charge, I d is the dark current, and S is the effective illumination area.

It is found from calculation based on formula that the R value and D* value of FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO device decreases with the increasing of power density, and the highest R and D* value of FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO device occurred at the light intensity of 0.32 mW cm−2. Therefore, it can be concluded that FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO device displays a high detection ability for photo-irradiation of low power density, suggesting a potential application of this device for UV detection of photo-irradiation at low power density. Furthermore, it implies an enhancement of photoresponse through the heterojunction between inorganic-organic materials and good hole transport of PEDOS.

Fig. 5e–f shows the I–V curves of FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO device in dark and light irradiation with different power densities. It can be seen from Fig. 5e(logarithmic I–V curve) that the device has good rectification characteristics, revealing the p-n junction behavior [57,58] At ± 1 V, the light current ratio is 1.43, while the dark current ratio is 2.42. In addition, the linear forward biased I–V curve (Fig. 5f) is nonlinear and asymmetric, demonstrating that a presence of p-n heterojunction structure in the device. It can be seen from Fig. 5f that the photocurrent increases gradually with the increasing of illumination intensity, and there is no tendency for the current to decrease, which is mainly due to the increase in the generation rate of electron-hole pairs under the irradiation of UV light with high illumination intensity. In Fig. 5g, we further evaluated the effect of power density on response time, where the rise time is 10%–90% of the maximum photocurrent, and the fall time is 90%–10% of the maximum photocurrent [59]. When the power density is increased from 0.32 mW cm−2 to 0.56 mW cm−2, the response time is faster, and then the response time is slower when the power density is increased from 0.56 mW cm−2 to 2.40 mW cm−2. This shows that an appropriate increase in power density will help to speed up the response time of the device, but further increase in power density will make the device produce more photogenerated carriers under strong power density, The separation and recombination process of these carriers results in a slower response time.

In order to discuss the pyroelectric effect induced by CQDs to lead an enhancement in UV detection performance of CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS device in detail, the photoresponses of different power densities are measured under 365 nm UV light irradiation. The I-t characteristic curves of the devices under light irradiation with different power densities are shown in Fig. 6 a. The I-t curves from 0.32 to 2.40 mW cm−2 in Fig. 6a display four-stage dynamic behavior. As presented in Fig. 6a–b, the I pyro + photo, I photo + thermo and relative peak-to-peak value (I pyro + photo–I pyro'+thermo’) increases both in the first and second stages with the increase of power density light, and the increase in the current of first stage is larger than that that of second stage. However, with the increase of power density, the change of I photo + thermo current is not obvious or even decreases. This is due to the fact that in the stage Ⅱ, I photo current is the main current, while the strong power density light will cause the shielding effect of photogenerated carriers in the CQDs-ZnO layer and the saturation of photovoltaic effect [26,27], so I photo + thermo does not show too much change with the increase of power density(Fig. 6b). This suggests that the pyroelectric effect from the incorporation of CQDs can enhance the current of the whole device. The maximum enhancement factors of (I pyro + photo–I pyro'+thermo')/I photo + thermo and I pyro + photo/I photo + thermo at low power density of 0.32 mW cm−2 and zero bias are 4.7 and 3.3, respectively. In order to better explain the performance enhancement mechanism of the pyroelectric effect on the device, the corresponding photoresponsivity (R) and specific detectivity (D*) under different power densities are calculated according to Equations (1) and (2), and the results are depicted in Fig. 6c–d. Under 365 nm illumination with a power density of 0.32 mW cm−2, Rphoto + thermo and D*photo + thermo are 4.47 mA W−1 and 1.23 × 1011 Jones, respectively. Under the same condition, for the Rpyro + photo–Rpyro'+thermo’ and Rpyro + photo are 22.62 mA W−1 and 15.70 mA W−1, respectively, which are 5 times and 3.5 times higher than Rphoto + thermo, respectively. Under the same conditions, the D*pyro + photo–D*pyro'+thermo’ and D*pyro + photo are 5.80 × 1011 Jones and 4.06 × 1011 Jones, respectively, which are 4.7 times and 3.3 times higher than D*photo + thermo, respectively. Compared with the self-powered UV photodetectors based on inorganic-organic heterojunctions reported in recent years, it has certain competitive advantages. The related photoelectric performances are listed in Table 1 . Therefore, all these results illustrate that the CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS display an excellent UV detection performance which means that it can be a candidate for the low-power UV light irradiation response.Moreover, all these results also reveal the enhancement of ZnO-based UV light detection through the synergy between the pyroelectric effect of CQDs and photocurrent effect from photoresponse of ZnO NRs/PEDOS.

Fig. 6e–f shows the I–V curves of FTO/CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO device in dark at different power densities. It can be observed from the logarithmic I–V curve in Fig. 6e that the device has good rectification characteristics. Furthermore, the linear forward biased I–V curves in Fig. 6f are nonlinear and asymmetric, indicating that the organic-inorganic UV photodetector forms a good p-n heterojunction. In addition, it can be seen that the photocurrent gradually increases with the illumination intensity, and there is no trend of decreasing the current(Fig. S4, Supporting Information), which is mainly due to the increase in the generation rate of electron-hole pairs under the irradiation of higher illumination intensity ultraviolet light. Compared with ZnO NRs/PEDOS, the current increase trend of CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS is relatively obvious, indicating that the CQDs are sensitive to the thermal radiation of UV light, and the combination of CQDs and ZnO NRs can cause thermoelectric effect which can highly improved the UV detection ability of device. In Fig. 6g, we further evaluated the influence of power density on the response time of FTO/CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO devices. The definitions of rise time and fall time are the same as those in Fig. 5g. But, the distance of rise time and fall time corresponding to different power densities of Fig. 6g (FTO/CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO) devices is significantly shorter than that of Fig. 5g (FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO) devices, which indicates that the modification with CQDs is helpful to adjust the response time of devices.

Fig. 7 shows the energy band diagram before and after p-n heterojunction formation of FTO/ZnO NRs/FTO and FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO devices. As shown in Fig. 7a, after ultraviolet light illumination, the incident photon energy is greater or equal to the band gap of ZnO, photogenerated carriers will be generated, and electrons will be excited from VB to CB of ZnO, thus generating photocurrent. However, defects in ZnO lattice will easily lead to recombination of photogenerated carriers [9,60,61], affecting the photoelectric performance of the device. In Fig. 7b, the energy band diagram of the FTO/ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO device shows that p-n heterojunction is formed between p-type PEDOS and n-type ZnO, which promotes the separation of photogenerated carriers under the effect of the electric field inside the junction area [27], so that electrons are collected through the ZnO layer to the FTO electrode, and holes are transferred to another FTO electrode through the PEDOS hole transport layer. Among them, the energy band offset between the LUMO of PEDOS and the CB of ZnO is 0.12 eV, while the energy band offset between the HOMO of PEDOS and the VB of ZnO is 2.02 eV, which effectively realizes the transfer of interface charge [57]. This interface charge transfer reduces the recombination of photogenerated carriers, thus improving the photoelectric performance of the device.

To better understand the experimental results in I-t curves (Fig. 4), we propose a mechanism for the pyroelectric and photovoltaic effects of the FTO/CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS/FTO device(Fig. 8 ). As displayed in Fig. 8a, there are temperature-induced thermal fluctuations that cause small-scale random oscillations of the electric dipoles on their respective axes, with high levels of polarization in the dark at room temperature [62,63]. Since ZnO NRs are crystals of wurtzite structure and belong to a kind of pyroelectric material [64], and ZnO NRs are prone to thermal deformation under temperature changes, resulting in the existence of free charges on the surface of the material and the generation of instantaneous potential [65,66]. Since there is no continuous change in temperature at room temperature (dT/dt = 0), the polarization of the electric dipole is constant. Therefore, no current is output. In the dark condition (Fig. 8b), there is not photo-generated carrier, and the photovoltaic effect of the device is zero. Therefore, the possible photoresponse mechanism for CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS in the dark is summarized as shown in Fig. 8c.

In case of UV light source (Fig. 8d), ZnO NRs and CQDs are easily heated by UV light radiation, especially CQDs are more likely to absorb UV light energy, causing the local temperature increase of the device (dT/dt > 0). Due to thermal vibrations, the electric dipoles oscillate to a greater extent around their respective alignment axes, resulting in a reduction in the amount of induced charge in the electrodes. Therefore, the thermal polarization of the electric dipole induces a pyroelectric effect, corresponding to a current of (I pyro and I thermo). In addition, the energy band gap of ZnO NRs is within the UV excitation wavelength, and a large number of UV-excited photogenerated carriers appear when illuminated (Fig. 8e). Although PEDOS cannot be excited by UV light(Fig. S5, Supporting Information), PEDOS is a good p-type hole transport material combines with n-type ZnO NRs to form a strong built-in electric field. Under the action of the built-in electric field, the photogenerated electrons and holes are separated by the electron transport layer(ZnO NRs) and the hole transport layer(PEDOS), and they finally transported to the FTO electrode to photocurrent (I photo). Accordingly, the possible photoresponse mechanism for CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS in the UV light irradiation is summarized as depicted in Fig. 8f. It can be observed from Fig. 8f that the total current of the device when illuminated is the current superposition effect caused by the photovoltaic effect and the pyroelectric effect.Consequently, the output current of stage I is (I pyro + photo). Since the transient temperature change quickly reaches a thermal equilibrium state, and I pyro disappears, but the temperature gradient of the device (dT/dt > 0) does not disappear immediately, and I thermo still exists. Therefore, the output current of the second stage is I photo + thermo. When the UV irradiation is turned off, the instantaneous temperature of the device is reduced (dT/dt < 0). Due to the lower thermal activity, the electric dipole oscillates within a smaller degree of diffusion angle. As a result, the amount of induced charge in the electrode increases, and the electric dipole is thermally polarized in the opposite direction(Fig. 8g), while the reverse output currents (I pyro’ and I thermo’) occur. In addition, as presented in Fig. 8h, the radiative photovoltaic effect without UV light is zero, and there is no continuous photogenerated carriers, and the photovoltaic effect output current(I photo) is zero. Accordingly, the possible photoresponse mechanism for CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS after UV radiation is turned off is summarized as depicted in Fig. 8i. Since there is no photovoltaic effect after UV radiation is turned off, there is current only caused by reverse pyroelectric effect, and the output current of stage III is I pyro'+thermo’. In stage IV, the transient thermal effect gradually stabilizes, and finally returns to the dark current(I dark) state when there is no light.

In summary, this paper cleverly uses the advantages of PEDOS and CQDs in optoelectronic materials to modify ZnO NRs, thereby solving the shortcomings of low responsivity and slow response time of ZnO-based UV PDs. As a hole transport material, the PEDOS realizes the rapid separation of electrons and holes under zero bias, thereby increasing the photocurrent of the UV PD. The CQDs easily absorb photon energy to bring a change in transient temperature for causing the pyroelectric effect, leading thermal polarization of electric charges under light irradiation to rapidly increase the instantaneous current of the UV PD. Therefore, a high-performance capability of ZnO-based self-powered UV PD is realized under the synergistic effect of PEDOS and CQDs.The photoresponse of CQDs-ZnO NRs/PEDOS is 33 times that of UV PD from pure ZnO NRs, and the response time is also shortened by half. The UV PD device achieves the advantages of high responsivity and short response time. In addition, the organic-inorganic face-to-face assembly method avoids polymer-encapsulation of ZnO NRs, consequently reduces energy loss from light irradiation. This work provides a theoretical basis for the future development of ZnO-based self-powered UV PDs.
